title: ST 312 — Introduction to Statistics II
description: The full ST 312 curriculum at NC State. Master inference for multiple samples, ANOVA, regression, categorical data analysis, and experimental design — the statistical toolkit for serious business and scientific analysis.
subject: Statistics

learn.openingQuestion: A DNA test correctly identifies criminals 99.9% of the time and gives false positives only 0.1% of the time. A suspect tests positive. A prosecutor tells the jury there's a 99.9% chance this person is guilty. What's actually wrong with that statement?
learn.anchor: Probability rules exist because our intuitions about uncertainty are systematically and catastrophically wrong — the same calculation that seems obvious in hindsight is nearly impossible to do correctly without the formal tools.
learn.anchorSummary: Our probability intuition is systematically biased; formal rules correct it.
learn.mechanism:
The prosecutor's fallacy has sent innocent people to prison. The error is confusing P(positive test | innocent) with P(innocent | positive test). These two probabilities sound similar but are completely different quantities — and mixing them up is one of the most consequential errors in applied probability.

Here's the correct calculation using Bayes' theorem. Suppose 1 in 10,000 people in the population could have committed this crime. Among 1 million potential suspects, that's 100 guilty parties. The test correctly identifies 99.9% of them — 99.9 guilty people flagged. Among the 999,900 innocent people, 0.1% get false positives — 1,000 innocent people flagged. So among the roughly 1,100 people who test positive, only about 100 are actually guilty. That's P(guilty | positive) ≈ 9%, not 99.9%.

Bayes' theorem formalizes this: P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B). The prosecutor conflated P(B|A) — probability of evidence given guilt — with P(A|B) — probability of guilt given evidence. The prior probability (how rare guilt is in the population) completely changes the answer. Beyond Bayes, the review covers the addition rule for disjoint events P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B); the general addition rule P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B); the multiplication rule for independent events P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B); and conditional probability P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B).

The deeper lesson: probability forces precision about the question you're actually asking. 'What's the probability of rain tomorrow' and 'what's the probability it was raining when the accident happened' are structurally different questions that require different probability machinery.
learn.mechanismSummary: Probability rules prevent systematic errors our intuition makes — especially around conditional probability and base rates.
learn.boundaryConditions: Classical probability assumes equally likely outcomes (rolling dice), which breaks down in complex real-world settings. Frequentist probability requires repeatable events — assigning probability to a one-time event like 'will this company be acquired?' requires Bayesian reasoning and careful prior elicitation. Bayes' theorem requires knowing or estimating the prior probability P(A), which is often controversial. When independence is assumed without verification, the multiplication rule produces wrong answers — many real-world events are correlated.
learn.example: COVID testing in 2020. A test with 99% sensitivity and 95% specificity used when prevalence is 1% (1 in 100 people infected). Among 10,000 people tested: 100 infected, test catches 99 of them. Among 9,900 uninfected, 5% = 495 get false positives. Total positives: 99 + 495 = 594. Positive predictive value = 99/594 = 16.7%. A positive test means only a 1-in-6 chance of being truly infected. This is why widespread testing with an imperfect test in a low-prevalence population produces mostly false positives — a fact that confused policymakers who focused on sensitivity without accounting for base rate.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Every probability calculation ultimately reduces to careful bookkeeping of conditional and unconditional probabilities. The rules aren't arbitrary — they're derived from the axioms of probability theory and they prevent the intuitive errors that lead to costly decisions. This framework underlies every hypothesis test and confidence interval in the course.
keyPoints: Confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A) is the prosecutor's fallacy — these are always different quantities unless P(A) = P(B); always specify which direction you're conditioning
Independence means P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) — this requires verification, not assumption; most real-world events are not independent
Base rates dominate when they're extreme — even a very accurate test produces mostly false positives in low-prevalence settings; always incorporate base rate into conditional probability reasoning

topics.title: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

learn.openingQuestion: Gallup polls 1,000 people and claims to know what 330 million Americans think, with only ±3% error. A local newspaper polls 200 of its readers and gets a different answer. Who's right — and why does Gallup's 1,000 tell us more than the newspaper's 200?
learn.anchor: The sampling distribution is the theoretical distribution of a statistic across all possible samples of size n — and the Central Limit Theorem guarantees it normalizes regardless of the population's shape, which is why any properly random sample of sufficient size lets you make precise population claims.
learn.anchorSummary: The CLT turns single-sample statistics into valid population inferences through predictable sampling behavior.
learn.mechanism:
Gallup wins for a structural reason that has nothing to do with how many people they asked. Polling 1,000 randomly selected Americans gives a margin of error of approximately ±3%. But polling 200 self-selected newspaper readers — regardless of newspaper circulation — gives a biased result, not because the sample is small but because self-selection violates random sampling. A biased sample of 10,000 is worse than an unbiased sample of 1,000. Sample size only reduces sampling variability; it cannot fix sampling bias.

The Central Limit Theorem states: regardless of the shape of the population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean x̄ approaches a normal distribution as n increases, with mean μ (the true population mean) and standard deviation σ/√n (called the standard error). For proportions, the sampling distribution of p̂ is approximately normal with mean p and standard deviation √(p(1−p)/n) for large n.

What 'sampling distribution' actually means: imagine taking 10,000 different random samples of 1,000 Americans and computing the sample proportion in each. Plot those 10,000 proportions. That distribution — the sampling distribution — is roughly normal, centered at the true value, with spread determined by n. We never actually take 10,000 samples; the CLT tells us what that distribution would look like mathematically. This is the theoretical foundation for all confidence intervals and hypothesis tests.

The standard error σ/√n tells us how much sample means vary around the true population mean. It decreases with √n — to cut the standard error in half, you need 4× the sample size. This law of diminishing returns explains why Gallup doesn't poll 10,000 people: going from 1,000 to 4,000 halves the margin of error, but that extra precision rarely changes conclusions enough to justify the cost.
learn.mechanismSummary: The CLT guarantees sample means are normally distributed around the true population mean; standard error = σ/√n and shrinks with √n, not n.
learn.boundaryConditions: The CLT requires 'large enough' n — roughly n ≥ 30 for approximately symmetric populations, larger for heavily skewed ones. For highly skewed distributions (income, wealth), n = 30 may not be sufficient and the normal approximation may be poor. The CLT applies to sums and means — NOT to the maximum or minimum of a sample. It also assumes independent, identically distributed observations. Stratified random sampling can be more efficient than simple random sampling by ensuring representation of subgroups, but requires knowing the strata structure.
learn.example: A factory produces light bulbs with mean life 1,200 hours and standard deviation 200 hours. Quality control samples n = 64 bulbs. The sampling distribution of x̄ is normal with mean 1,200 and standard error 200/√64 = 25 hours. If the QC team observes x̄ = 1,260 hours: Z = (1,260 − 1,200)/25 = 2.4, p = 0.016. Unusual enough to investigate whether the process has shifted — and this inference works without knowing the shape of the individual bulb-lifetime distribution, because n = 64 is large enough for the CLT.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: The sampling distribution is what makes all of classical statistics work. Without it, a single sample tells you only about those specific observations. With it, the single sample becomes evidence about a population through the predictable mathematical behavior of sample statistics. Every formula in this course — confidence intervals, t-tests, F-tests, chi-square tests — is built on the sampling distribution concept.
keyPoints: Standard error (σ/√n) measures how much a statistic varies across samples — it is NOT the same as the population standard deviation σ; confusing them is one of the most common errors in applied statistics
The CLT applies to the sample mean — not to individual observations, which retain the shape of the population distribution regardless of n
Sample size reduces variance; biased sampling cannot be fixed by increasing n — a biased sample of 1 million is worse than an unbiased sample of 1,000

learn.openingQuestion: A political poll shows 52% for Candidate A with a ±3% margin of error. A TV anchor says 'there's a 95% chance Candidate A is leading.' The anchor sounds authoritative. What did they fundamentally misunderstand about confidence intervals?
learn.anchor: A 95% confidence interval means the procedure produces intervals that contain the true parameter 95% of the time across many applications — NOT that there's a 95% chance the specific interval you computed contains the true value.
learn.anchorSummary: 95% CI describes the procedure's long-run reliability, not the probability about this specific interval.
learn.mechanism:
The TV anchor's error is subtle but fundamental. Once you've computed a specific interval — say [49%, 55%] — the true population proportion is either in that interval or it isn't. There's no randomness left; it's a fixed unknown fact. Saying 'there's a 95% chance the true proportion is between 49% and 55%' treats the true proportion as if it's random. It's not. The interval is what's random — it changes every time you take a new sample.

The correct interpretation: if we built confidence intervals using this procedure across 100 different polls of 1,000 people each, approximately 95 of those 100 intervals would contain the true population proportion. This specific interval is one of those 100 — and we don't know if it's one of the 95 that worked or one of the 5 that didn't. That's the honest statement.

The formula: point estimate ± (critical value) × (standard error). For a proportion: p̂ ± z* × √(p̂(1−p̂)/n), where z* = 1.96 for 95%, z* = 2.576 for 99%. For a mean with unknown σ: x̄ ± t* × (s/√n), where t* depends on degrees of freedom n−1. Confidence level and width trade off directly: a 99% CI is wider than a 95% CI because you cast a wider net to be more sure of capturing the true value.

You cannot have both a narrow interval AND high confidence without a larger sample size — which is why researchers pre-specify the acceptable width and then calculate the required n. For the poll: margin of error = 1.96 × √(0.52 × 0.48/1,000) = 1.96 × 0.0158 = 3.1%. The interval [49%, 55%] was built using a procedure that works 95% of the time — not a guarantee for this specific interval.
learn.mechanismSummary: Confidence intervals quantify estimation uncertainty using a procedure with known long-run reliability; width trades off against confidence level, and both improve with larger n.
learn.boundaryConditions: Confidence intervals require approximately normal sampling distribution — verified by CLT conditions. They assume a properly random sample; selection bias or non-response bias makes the CI meaningless regardless of width. Extremely small samples (n < 15) with non-normal populations make t-based CIs unreliable. CIs for parameters other than means and proportions (medians, correlations, regression slopes) require different formulas. A CI that excludes zero (or some null value) is consistent with rejecting H₀ at the corresponding α — this duality is exact.
learn.example: A pharmaceutical study measures blood pressure reduction in n = 25 patients: x̄ = 8.2 mmHg, s = 3.1 mmHg. The 95% CI uses t*(24 df) = 2.064: 8.2 ± 2.064 × (3.1/√25) = 8.2 ± 1.28 = [6.92, 9.48]. Since the entire interval is above 0, this is evidence the drug reduces blood pressure. But a separate clinical question: is a 6.92–9.48 mmHg reduction meaningful for patients? That question is not answered by the CI — it requires clinical judgment about what magnitude of reduction matters.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Confidence intervals formalize what it means to 'estimate with uncertainty' — replacing a single guess with a range that has known long-run reliability. Every CI has three components: a point estimate, a margin of error, and a confidence level, and understanding all three is required for correct interpretation. The CI and the hypothesis test are two sides of the same inference coin: a 95% CI excludes the null value if and only if the corresponding two-sided test rejects at α = 0.05.
keyPoints: '95% confident' describes the procedure, not the probability that this specific interval contains the parameter — the parameter is fixed, not random; this is the most important conceptual distinction in inference
Wider CI = more confidence OR smaller n — you cannot increase confidence without paying with width, sample size, or both
The margin of error is ± half the interval width; a poll showing 52% ±3% means the interval is [49%, 55%], not a guarantee the candidate leads

learn.openingQuestion: A drug reduces blood pressure by an average of 1 mmHg. A clinical trial of 10,000 patients shows p = 0.001. The drug gets FDA approval. Should doctors prescribe it — and is a 1 mmHg reduction actually worth anything to a patient?
learn.anchor: A p-value measures how surprising your data would be if the null hypothesis were true — it says nothing about the size or importance of an effect, only whether the data are consistent with no effect at all.
learn.anchorSummary: p-values measure statistical surprise, not effect size or practical importance.
learn.mechanism:
The drug example is a real pattern. With 10,000 patients, the study has enormous statistical power — it can detect even tiny effects as 'significant.' A 1 mmHg reduction in blood pressure is statistically real (unlikely due to chance) but clinically trivial — normal blood pressure variation throughout the day is 10–20 mmHg. The p-value of 0.001 correctly tells you 'this effect exists in the population.' It says nothing about whether the effect is large enough to matter clinically, justify the cost, or warrant the side effects.

The hypothesis testing framework: State H₀ (null hypothesis — typically 'no effect') and Hₐ (alternative). Collect data and compute the test statistic. Find the p-value = P(getting a test statistic at least as extreme as observed | H₀ is true). If p < α, reject H₀. If p ≥ α, fail to reject H₀.

What the p-value is NOT: not the probability that H₀ is true; not the probability that the results are due to chance; not the probability of making an error; not the probability that the study will replicate. What it IS: the probability of observing data as extreme as what was observed, or more extreme, assuming H₀ is true. This distinction matters enormously — a p-value of 0.03 does not mean there's a 3% chance your null hypothesis is true.

The significance level α is set before the experiment — typically 0.05. Two-sided tests check for any difference (Hₐ: μ₁ ≠ μ₂); one-sided tests check for a specific direction (Hₐ: μ₁ > μ₂). One-sided tests have more power when direction is pre-specified, but retroactively choosing one-sided after seeing the data direction is p-hacking and invalidates the inference.
learn.mechanismSummary: Hypothesis tests quantify evidence against the null using p-values — but statistical significance never implies practical significance, especially with large n.
learn.boundaryConditions: Hypothesis testing assumes a properly random sample. The 'reject at p < 0.05' threshold is conventional, not mathematical truth — many fields now use 0.005 or simply report effect sizes and confidence intervals alongside p-values. P-values are easily manipulated through optional stopping, multiple testing, and post-hoc hypothesis revision (p-hacking). A non-significant result with low power is uninformative — it may mean no effect, or it may mean the study was underpowered. Always report effect sizes alongside p-values.
learn.example: Testing whether a new teaching method improves exam scores. H₀: μ_new − μ_old = 0 vs. Hₐ: μ_new − μ_old ≠ 0. n = 40 students per group. Results: x̄_new = 78.2, x̄_old = 76.4, s_pooled = 8.1, t = 1.08, p = 0.28. Fail to reject H₀. But the 95% CI is [−1.2, 4.8] — consistent with improvements of up to 4.8 points. The study may simply be underpowered to detect a meaningful difference. 'Fail to reject' does not prove the means are equal; it only means this sample size couldn't distinguish the difference from chance.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Hypothesis testing is a decision framework with controlled error rates — not a truth-detection machine. The p-value measures only one specific kind of evidence (against the null), and interpreting it requires knowing sample size, effect size, and the practical stakes of being wrong in either direction. Every specific test (t, F, chi-square) is an application of this framework with a test statistic tailored to the parameter being tested.
keyPoints: A significant p-value with a huge sample doesn't mean the effect is important; a non-significant p-value with a tiny sample doesn't mean there's no effect — always consider sample size and effect size together
Never 'accept' the null hypothesis — we only 'fail to reject' it; absence of statistical evidence is not evidence of absence of the effect

P-hacking (testing multiple outcomes, stopping when p < 0.05, switching to one-sided after seeing the data direction) produces false positives systematically — pre-registration of analysis plans exists to prevent it

learn.openingQuestion: A company analyzes pay data and finds male managers average $72,000 and female managers average $67,000. Is this a $5,000 pay gap that demands investigation — or is it noise that would disappear if they hired different managers next year?
learn.anchor: Two-sample inference separates real systematic differences between groups from the random variation you'd see in any two samples even if the groups were identical at the population level.
learn.anchorSummary: Two-sample tests ask: could this group difference plausibly be random sampling variation?
learn.mechanism:
The $5,000 gap is real data, but the question is whether it reflects a systematic population-level pattern or sampling variation. If the company has 50 male and 50 female managers selected somewhat randomly from a larger pool, you'd expect some gap just by chance — sample means won't perfectly equal the population means. Two-sample inference quantifies how large a gap would be 'unusual' if there were truly no systematic difference.

The two-sample t-test: H₀: μ₁ − μ₂ = 0 vs. Hₐ: μ₁ − μ₂ ≠ 0. Test statistic: t = (x̄₁ − x̄₂) / SE, where SE = √(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂) under Welch's procedure (unequal variances — the default). Degrees of freedom are approximated by the Welch-Satterthwaite formula, which software computes automatically.

For the pay gap: n_male = 50, x̄_male = $72,000, s_male = $12,000; n_female = 50, x̄_female = $67,000, s_female = $11,000. SE = √(12,000²/50 + 11,000²/50) = √(2,880,000 + 2,420,000) = $2,302. t = 5,000/2,302 = 2.17, p = 0.032. Reject H₀ at α = 0.05. The 95% CI for the gap: [$376, $9,624]. The gap is statistically real — but this is observational data, so it doesn't establish that the company discriminates. Confounders (years of experience, department, role type) could explain the difference.

Paired vs. independent: Two samples are independent when different subjects appear in each group. They're paired when the same subjects are measured twice (before/after) or when subjects are explicitly matched on relevant characteristics. Paired data must be analyzed as a one-sample t-test on the differences — not as two independent groups. Analyzing paired data as independent loses statistical power and can give wrong answers.
learn.mechanismSummary: Welch's two-sample t-test compares group means against sampling variability; the CI shows the range of plausible true differences; paired data require paired analysis.
learn.boundaryConditions: Welch's t assumes independent observations and approximately normal distributions within each group (or large enough n for CLT). The pooled t-test assumes equal variances and should only be used when verified — Welch's is robust to unequal variances and is the safe default. The test doesn't control for confounders in observational studies; a significant result means the groups differ, not why. For proportions, the two-proportion z-test uses the pooled proportion for hypothesis testing but unpooled SE for confidence intervals — a common source of confusion.
learn.example: A/B testing a website checkout layout. Old layout: 120 purchases out of 800 visitors (15%). New layout: 144 purchases out of 800 visitors (18%). Two-proportion z-test: pooled p̂ = 264/1,600 = 0.165. SE = √(0.165 × 0.835 × (1/800 + 1/800)) = 0.01853. z = (0.18 − 0.15)/0.01853 = 1.62, p = 0.105. Fail to reject — the 3-percentage-point improvement is not statistically distinguishable from chance with 800 visitors per group. This is a common A/B testing outcome: real-seeming differences that aren't statistically significant with typical sample sizes.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Two-sample inference is the workhorse of comparative studies — from clinical trials to market research to pay equity audits. The framework is always the same: is the observed difference between groups larger than what chance variation would predict if the groups were truly identical? The answer requires comparing the observed difference to its standard error — the fundamental logic of every test statistic in statistics.
keyPoints: Paired vs. independent is determined by the study design, not your preference — the same person measured twice is always paired; using independent methods on paired data is always wrong and typically loses power
Welch's t-test (unequal variances) is the default; only use the pooled t when you've explicitly verified approximately equal variances
Statistical significance in an observational study doesn't imply causation — a significant pay gap finding requires further analysis of confounders before concluding discrimination

learn.openingQuestion: You're comparing 5 fertilizers on crop yield and decide to run pairwise t-tests between all 10 pairs. Your statistics professor groans. Before you've even seen the results, what has already gone wrong?
learn.anchor: ANOVA tests whether any group means differ by decomposing total variation into between-group signal and within-group noise — and it does so in a single test that keeps the false positive rate under control, unlike running multiple t-tests.
learn.anchorSummary: ANOVA compares k group means simultaneously without inflating Type I error through multiple testing.
learn.mechanism:
The 10-pairwise-t-test problem: with 10 independent tests each at α = 0.05, the probability of at least one false positive is 1 − 0.95^10 = 0.40. You have a 40% chance of declaring some fertilizer 'significantly better' even if all five are identical. ANOVA solves this by testing all k means simultaneously with a single test that maintains overall Type I error at α.

The F statistic is the ratio of two variance estimates: F = MSG/MSE. MSG (Mean Square for Groups) measures how much group means vary from the grand mean. If all group means are equal (H₀ is true), MSG estimates σ² — the random variation within groups. If groups have different means (H₀ is false), MSG overestimates σ², making F large. MSE (Mean Square for Error) always estimates σ² regardless of whether H₀ is true. It pools within-group variance across all groups: MSE = SS_error/(N − k).

The ANOVA table: Source Groups (df = k−1, MS = SS_groups/(k−1), F = MSG/MSE); Source Error (df = N−k, MS = SS_error/(N−k)); Source Total (df = N−1). SS_total = SS_groups + SS_error. Three assumptions: independence of observations; approximately normal distributions within each group (or large n for CLT); equal variances across groups, checked by verifying the largest group SD is less than 2× the smallest.

For a cereal shelf example: k = 3 locations, N = 16 cereals. SS_groups = 0.2715, SS_error = 0.1770. MSG = 0.2715/2 = 0.1358. MSE = 0.1770/13 = 0.0136. F = 0.1358/0.0136 = 9.99. With df = (2, 13), p = 0.002. Strong evidence that mean sugar content differs across at least one pair of shelf locations.
learn.mechanismSummary: F = between-group variance / within-group variance; a large F means group differences are too large to attribute to within-group noise alone.
learn.boundaryConditions: ANOVA requires equal variance (homoscedasticity); Welch's ANOVA handles unequal variances. For non-normal data in small samples, the Kruskal-Wallis nonparametric test is an alternative. A significant ANOVA F test tells you at least one mean differs — it does NOT identify which ones; post-hoc tests are required for pairwise follow-up. A non-significant result means no evidence any means differ, not that all means are equal — the study may simply be underpowered.
learn.example: An NC State experiment tests 4 soybean varieties on yield (bu/acre). n = 5 plots per variety. Means: [38.2, 41.5, 39.8, 43.1]. Grand mean = 40.65. SS_groups = 5 × [(38.2−40.65)² + (41.5−40.65)² + (39.8−40.65)² + (43.1−40.65)²] = 5 × [6.003 + 0.723 + 0.723 + 6.003] = 67.25. df_groups = 3, MSG = 22.42. If MSE = 4.2 (from within-variety variation), F = 22.42/4.2 = 5.34, p = 0.007. Reject H₀ — evidence that at least one variety yields differently. Post-hoc tests are needed to identify which varieties differ.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: ANOVA is what happens when the two-sample t-test's logic scales to multiple groups. The insight that 'comparing between-group variation to within-group variation' works whether you have 2 groups or 20 makes F the universal test for comparing means. The two-sample t-test is a special case of ANOVA with k = 2 groups: F = t² when there are exactly two groups.
keyPoints: A significant ANOVA F only tells you 'at least one mean differs' — it does NOT identify which means are different; always follow with post-hoc tests to find out which ones
The equal variance assumption must be checked: largest group SD < 2× smallest SD is the quick rule; violating this makes F unreliable

Running multiple t-tests instead of ANOVA inflates familywise Type I error — with 5 groups and 10 pairwise tests at α = 0.05, you have a 40% chance of a false positive even when all groups are identical

learn.openingQuestion: An ANOVA shows your 4 fertilizer groups are 'significantly different' at p = 0.002. Your boss wants to know which fertilizers outperform which. You run all 6 pairwise t-tests at α = 0.05 and find 3 significant differences. Why is your statistics professor still not satisfied?
learn.anchor: Post-hoc tests solve the multiple comparisons problem by adjusting significance thresholds so that the overall false positive rate stays controlled across all pairwise tests combined — not just within each individual test.
learn.anchorSummary: Post-hoc tests adjust per-comparison thresholds so overall false positive rate stays at α across all comparisons.
learn.mechanism:
The problem reappears even after a significant ANOVA. With k = 4 groups, there are C(4,2) = 6 pairwise comparisons. Running 6 t-tests unadjusted at α = 0.05 gives a familywise error rate of 1 − (0.95)^6 = 0.265 — over a quarter chance of at least one false positive even if all groups truly have the same mean. The significant ANOVA F test told you some differences exist; the post-hoc tests need to tell you which ones while maintaining control over the overall error rate.

Bonferroni correction: use αᵢ = α_familywise / m, where m is the number of comparisons. For 6 comparisons at α = 0.05: αᵢ = 0.05/6 = 0.0083 per test. This guarantees familywise error ≤ 0.05. It's simple and applies to any collection of tests, but it's conservative (overly strict) when many comparisons are planned.

Tukey's HSD (Honestly Significant Difference): designed specifically for all pairwise comparisons after ANOVA. Uses the studentized range distribution rather than the t-distribution. For each pair: |x̄ᵢ − x̄ⱼ| > q*(k, N−k) × √(MSE/n) to declare significance. Tukey's is more powerful than Bonferroni when making all pairwise comparisons and has exact familywise error control.

Fisher's LSD: the least conservative option — requires a significant overall ANOVA F, then runs unadjusted pairwise comparisons. Only valid for exactly 3 groups (where familywise error happens to be controlled); for k > 3, it's too liberal and should not be used. Planned contrasts: hypotheses specified before data collection require less correction than exploratory post-hoc analysis — using the data to decide which contrasts to test invalidates this exemption.
learn.mechanismSummary: Bonferroni divides α by number of tests (universal but conservative); Tukey uses the studentized range distribution (more powerful specifically for all pairwise comparisons after ANOVA).
learn.boundaryConditions: Post-hoc tests assume all ANOVA assumptions hold. Bonferroni becomes extremely conservative with 20+ comparisons, making real effects hard to detect. Tukey requires equal or near-equal sample sizes for maximum accuracy; Games-Howell is an alternative for unequal variances. When comparing all treatments to a single control group (not all pairwise), Dunnett's test is more powerful than Tukey. Planned contrasts can be tested without Bonferroni adjustment only when truly pre-specified — using data to decide which contrasts to test invalidates this exemption.
learn.example: ANOVA for 3 fertilizer types (standard, organic, slow-release): F(2,27) = 5.8, p = 0.008. MSE = 15.6, n = 10 per group. Means: standard = 42.1, organic = 47.3, slow-release = 44.8 bu/acre. Tukey q* for k = 3, df = 27 at α = 0.05 ≈ 3.51. Tukey critical difference = 3.51 × √(15.6/10) = 3.51 × 1.249 = 4.38 bu/acre. |organic − standard| = 5.2 > 4.38 → significant. |slow-release − standard| = 2.7 < 4.38 → not significant. |organic − slow-release| = 2.5 < 4.38 → not significant. Conclusion: only organic fertilizer significantly outperforms standard; slow-release is not distinguishable from either.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Multiple comparisons is not just an ANOVA problem — it arises whenever many hypotheses are tested simultaneously (genetic association studies, A/B tests on multiple metrics, subgroup analyses in clinical trials). The principle is universal: each additional test increases false positive risk, and the solution is always some form of adjustment. The choice of method (Bonferroni, Tukey, FDR) depends on the structure of the comparisons and the acceptable error rate.
keyPoints: Never run multiple unadjusted t-tests after ANOVA — even with a significant F, each pairwise comparison needs adjustment for multiple comparisons
Use Tukey for all pairwise comparisons after ANOVA; Bonferroni for mixed collections of tests not all pairwise; Fisher's LSD only for exactly k = 3 groups
Planned contrasts specified before seeing data require less adjustment than data-driven post-hoc comparisons — this is why pre-registration of analysis plans matters

learn.openingQuestion: A drug works brilliantly for patients under 50 but makes patients over 50 measurably worse. If you pool all ages together in a single analysis, you find 'no significant drug effect.' You've just given wrong medical advice to everyone. What structural error in the analysis caused this?
learn.anchor: Two-way ANOVA reveals interactions — situations where the effect of one factor depends on the level of another — which a one-way analysis would hide by averaging over subgroups and producing a misleading 'no effect' conclusion.
learn.anchorSummary: Two-way ANOVA tests main effects and their interaction — always test interaction before interpreting main effects.
learn.mechanism:
The drug-age interaction scenario is not hypothetical. The Women's Health Initiative (2002) overturned decades of evidence about hormone replacement therapy because early studies pooled all women. Randomized trials later showed HRT reduced heart disease risk in younger women but increased it in older women. Pooling hid the interaction between treatment and age — the 'no effect' finding was real as an average, but it was a meaningless average.

Two-way ANOVA tests three hypotheses simultaneously. H₀_A: no main effect of A (all A-level means equal, averaged over B). H₀_B: no main effect of B (all B-level means equal, averaged over A). H₀_AB: no interaction (the effect of A is the same at every level of B). Always test interaction first. If the interaction is significant, main effects cannot be interpreted in isolation — the 'main effect of drug' means nothing if its direction depends on age.

Interaction plots: plot means on the y-axis, one factor on the x-axis, separate lines for each level of the other factor. Parallel lines → no interaction. Non-parallel (converging, diverging, crossing) lines → interaction. Crossing lines indicate a disordinal interaction — the ranking of groups actually reverses. ANOVA table sources for balanced design: Factor A (df = a−1), Factor B (df = b−1), Interaction A×B (df = (a−1)(b−1)), Error (df = ab(n−1)), Total (df = abn−1).

Drug example: Drug type (a = 2) and age group (b = 2), n = 25 per cell. Cell means: Drug/Under 50 = 75, Drug/Over 50 = 45, Placebo/Under 50 = 50, Placebo/Over 50 = 55. Drug effect for under 50: 75 − 50 = +25. Drug effect for over 50: 45 − 55 = −10. The effect flips sign — strong disordinal interaction. The 'main effect' of drug = (+25 + (−10))/2 = +7.5 — a meaningless average that describes no actual patient's experience.
learn.mechanismSummary: Test interaction first; if significant, main effects are misleading without qualification. Interaction means Factor A's effect depends on Factor B's level.
learn.boundaryConditions: Two-way ANOVA assumes independence, normality within cells, and equal variance across cells. For unbalanced designs (unequal n per cell), Type I, II, and III sums of squares give different results — software typically defaults to Type III, which is correct for unbalanced designs. Missing cells make interaction unestimable. If the interaction is not significant, main effects can be interpreted in isolation. Don't over-interpret a non-significant interaction test as proof of no interaction — the study may be underpowered to detect it.
learn.example: Studying the effect of coffee (yes/no) and time of day (morning/afternoon) on reaction time (ms). n = 8 per cell. Cell means: Coffee/Morning = 350, Coffee/Afternoon = 380, NoCoffee/Morning = 410, NoCoffee/Afternoon = 400. Coffee benefit in morning = 410 − 350 = 60 ms; coffee benefit in afternoon = 400 − 380 = 20 ms. These differ — the benefit of coffee is three times larger in the morning. Interaction F(1,28) = 4.2, p = 0.05 — significant. The story requires both factors to tell: coffee's benefit depends on the time of day.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Every interaction is a story about context-dependency — when the effect of X on Y depends on Z, no simple summary of X's effect exists. Two-way ANOVA is the formal tool for detecting and quantifying this context-dependency. The logic extends to three-way ANOVA and to regression with interaction terms (X₁ × X₂), which are the same phenomenon in a different mathematical form.
keyPoints: A significant interaction means main effects cannot be interpreted in isolation — examine simple effects (A's effect within each level of B) instead of main effects
Parallel lines in an interaction plot = no interaction; non-parallel, converging, or crossing lines = interaction; learn to draw interaction plots from cell means and read the pattern
Interaction df = (a−1)(b−1): a 2×2 design has 1 df for interaction; a 2×4 design has (1)(3) = 3 df for interaction

units.title: Unit 4: Correlation and Simple Linear Regression

learn.openingQuestion: For 20 years, ice cream sales and drowning rates move together almost perfectly — r = 0.85, p < 0.001. A public health researcher concludes that ice cream causes drowning. Why is this not just a logic problem but a statistics problem?
learn.anchor: The Pearson correlation coefficient measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship — testing its significance separates real linear association from coincidental co-movement in a specific sample, but a significant correlation cannot distinguish causation from a shared lurking variable.
learn.anchorSummary: Correlation inference tests whether linear association is real; significance never implies causation.
learn.mechanism:
Ice cream and drowning are both driven by warm weather. In summer, both increase; in winter, both decrease. The correlation is statistically real — the pattern is not due to sampling chance — but it's spurious. Both variables share a common cause (temperature/season) rather than causing each other. Correlation measures co-movement; it cannot distinguish direct causal relationships from shared common causes. This is not a statistical limitation that better tests could fix — it's a fundamental limit of observational correlation analysis.

The Pearson correlation coefficient r = Σ[(xᵢ − x̄)(yᵢ − ȳ)] / [(n−1)sₓsᵧ] ranges from −1 (perfect negative linear association) to +1 (perfect positive linear association). r = 0 means no linear association — but a nonlinear relationship could still exist. Always check the scatterplot before interpreting r.

Inference for correlation: H₀: ρ = 0 (no linear association in the population) vs. Hₐ: ρ ≠ 0. Test statistic: t = r√(n−2)/√(1−r²), with n−2 degrees of freedom. For r = 0.85 and n = 20: t = 0.85 × √18 / √(1−0.7225) = 0.85 × 4.243 / 0.527 = 6.85, p < 0.001. Highly significant — the correlation is real in the population, not just sampling noise. That verdict says nothing about causation.

The coefficient of determination r² = 0.85² = 0.72 means 72% of variability in drowning rates is 'explained' by ice cream sales in the statistical sense of predicted by a linear model. The word 'explained' is technical, not causal. A single influential outlier can dramatically change r — always verify with a scatterplot.
learn.mechanismSummary: t = r√(n−2)/√(1−r²) tests H₀: ρ = 0; significant result confirms real linear association in the population, not causation.
learn.boundaryConditions: Pearson r measures only linear association — a perfect quadratic relationship can have r near 0. Both variables must be quantitative and continuous. r is not resistant to outliers — a single extreme point can make a nonexistent pattern look strong or a real pattern disappear. The t-test for correlation formally assumes bivariate normality. For ordinal data or heavily non-normal distributions, Spearman's rank correlation is an alternative. Fisher's z-transformation is needed to construct confidence intervals for ρ.
learn.example: Does higher SAT score correlate with college GPA? n = 150 students: r = 0.41. t = 0.41 × √148 / √(1−0.1681) = 0.41 × 12.166 / 0.912 = 5.47, p < 0.001. Significant evidence of a positive linear association. But r² = 0.168 — only 16.8% of variability in GPA is explained by SAT score. Study habits, course difficulty, and motivation account for the remaining 83.2%. A significant correlation with r² = 0.17 is real but weak — the SAT is a poor individual predictor of GPA despite a statistically significant relationship.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Inference for correlation is the first step in regression analysis — testing H₀: ρ = 0 is equivalent to testing H₀: β₁ = 0 in simple linear regression. The correlation describes association strength and direction; regression builds on this to quantify exactly how much Y changes per unit of X, enabling prediction. The relationship between r and the regression slope b₁ is exact: b₁ = r × (sᵧ/sₓ).
keyPoints: A significant correlation confirms the association exists in the population — it says nothing about causation; always consider lurking variables in observational data
r measures only linear relationships — r near 0 does not mean no relationship, it means no linear relationship; always examine the scatterplot before interpreting r
r² (not r) is the proportion of variation in Y explained by X; r = 0.5 explains only 25% of variation — students routinely confuse r and r²

learn.openingQuestion: A real estate algorithm claims each additional square foot adds $127 to a home's value. A house with a large finished basement shows up as worth $40,000 less than predicted — despite the basement being a genuine asset. How can a model that explains 82% of price variation be so systematically wrong about this house?
learn.anchor: The least squares regression line is the unique line that minimizes total squared prediction error — which makes it optimally precise on average, but systematic errors occur for any subgroup the single-slope model doesn't separately represent.
learn.anchorSummary: Least squares minimizes total squared error, giving the best-on-average line that can still be wrong for specific subgroups.
learn.mechanism:
The basement example reveals what regression does and doesn't do. The algorithm learned that on average, larger houses sell for more money. But finished basements add square footage differently than above-grade living space — buyers typically value below-grade space less per square foot. The slope of $127/sq ft is the average relationship across all home types; it's systematically wrong for basements. This isn't a flaw in the math — it's what happens when a single slope is imposed on heterogeneous data.

The simple linear regression model: Y = β₀ + β₁X + ε, where β₀ is the y-intercept, β₁ is the slope, and ε ~ N(0, σ²) is random error. We estimate β₀ and β₁ from data by minimizing the sum of squared residuals: Σ(yᵢ − ŷᵢ)². The least squares estimates are b₁ = r × (sᵧ/sₓ) and b₀ = ȳ − b₁x̄. The least squares line always passes through (x̄, ȳ) and the sum of residuals always equals zero.

Slope interpretation: for each one-unit increase in X, the predicted value of Y changes by b₁ units, on average. The 'on average' qualifier is critical — it's not a guarantee for any individual. R² = r² in simple regression — the proportion of variability in Y explained by the linear relationship with X. R² = 0.82 means 82% of price variation is captured by square footage, but the remaining 18% includes location, age, renovation quality, and basement type.

Danger of extrapolation: the regression model describes the relationship within the observed range of X. Extending predictions far outside that range assumes the linear relationship continues — an unjustified assumption that can produce absurd predictions. The intercept is often meaningless (what does a 0-square-foot house cost?) unless 0 is near the data range.
learn.mechanismSummary: b₁ = r×(sᵧ/sₓ) is the slope; b₀ = ȳ − b₁x̄ is the intercept; R² measures proportion of Y variation explained; the line always passes through (x̄, ȳ).
learn.boundaryConditions: Simple linear regression assumes linearity (check scatterplot), independence of observations, normality of residuals, and equal variance (check residual plots). It is sensitive to outliers and influential points — always examine the data visually. Extrapolation beyond the data range is dangerous. Correlation does not imply causation: a significant regression doesn't mean X causes Y. The model assumes a single slope for all values of X — if the relationship differs for subgroups, a single regression misrepresents all of them.
learn.example: Predicting exam score (Y) from hours studied (X). n = 30 students. x̄ = 4.2 hours, ȳ = 74.8 points, sₓ = 1.8 hours, sᵧ = 12.3 points, r = 0.71. b₁ = 0.71 × (12.3/1.8) = 4.85 points/hour. b₀ = 74.8 − 4.85(4.2) = 54.43. Equation: ŷ = 54.43 + 4.85x. Predicted score for 5 hours studied: 54.43 + 4.85(5) = 78.7. R² = 0.71² = 0.504 — 50% of score variability is explained by study time. Residual for a student who studied 5 hours and scored 71: 71 − 78.7 = −7.7 points.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Simple linear regression takes correlation — a single number measuring association — and turns it into a model with a specific slope and intercept. The slope has an actionable interpretation (every additional hour studied predicts 4.85 more points) that correlation alone doesn't provide. This model is the foundation for all the inference, diagnostics, and extensions in the next several topics.
keyPoints: Interpret the slope as 'average change in Y per one-unit increase in X' — in multiple regression this becomes 'holding all other predictors constant,' which fundamentally changes the interpretation
R² is the proportion of variability in Y explained by X — NOT the probability that a specific prediction is correct; a high R² doesn't mean individual predictions are accurate
Never extrapolate beyond the range of the X data — the linear relationship may be completely wrong outside the observed range, and the intercept is often uninterpretable

learn.openingQuestion: A hedge fund builds a model predicting next-day stock returns from trading volume. Slope b₁ = 0.0023, p = 0.003 — highly significant. R² = 0.08. They plan to bet $100 million on it. Should p = 0.003 make you confident in this trade?
learn.anchor: Regression inference separates the systematic signal in a slope from random noise — but statistical significance and predictive usefulness are completely different things, and a model can be highly significant while explaining almost nothing.
learn.anchorSummary: Significance tests whether the slope is real; R² tells you how useful the model actually is — they are independent.
learn.mechanism:
The hedge fund's dilemma: with a large dataset (say, 5,000 trading days), the slope b₁ = 0.0023 is definitively not zero — the relationship between trading volume and next-day returns is real in the population. But R² = 0.08 means trading volume explains only 8% of next-day return variability. The remaining 92% is noise from the model's perspective. A real relationship and a useful relationship are completely different things.

Inference for the slope: H₀: β₁ = 0 vs. Hₐ: β₁ ≠ 0. Test statistic: t = b₁ / SE_b₁, with n−2 degrees of freedom. SE_b₁ = √(MSE / Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²). The regression ANOVA table: Source Regression (SS = Σ(ŷᵢ−ȳ)², df = 1); Source Error (SS = Σ(yᵢ−ŷᵢ)², df = n−2). In simple regression, the F test for the model and the t test for the slope test the same hypothesis: F = t².

Confidence interval for mean response vs. prediction interval for individual: CI for mean at x*: ŷ ± t* × s√(1/n + (x*−x̄)²/Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²). PI for individual at x*: ŷ ± t* × s√(1 + 1/n + (x*−x̄)²/Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²). The prediction interval is always wider because it accounts for both uncertainty in estimating the line AND individual-to-individual variation around the line. The CI estimates 'where is the average Y for all subjects with this X?' The PI estimates 'what will this one specific subject's Y be?' Both intervals are narrowest at x* = x̄ and widen as x* moves away from the center of the data.
learn.mechanismSummary: t = b₁/SE_b₁ tests whether slope is nonzero; CI estimates mean response at x*; PI estimates individual response — PI is always wider because it includes individual variation around the line.
learn.boundaryConditions: Regression inference requires the LINE assumptions (Linearity, Independence, Normality of residuals, Equal variance) — check residual plots before reporting results. With very large n, trivially small slopes become statistically significant — always check R² alongside p-value to assess practical importance. Prediction intervals extrapolated beyond the data range are especially unreliable. In simple regression F = t², but in multiple regression the overall F tests all slopes simultaneously while individual t-tests test each slope separately.
learn.example: Predicting final exam score (Y) from midterm score (X). n = 40, b₁ = 0.82, SE_b₁ = 0.12. t = 0.82/0.12 = 6.83, p < 0.001. R² = 0.55. 95% CI for β₁: 0.82 ± 2.024 × 0.12 = [0.577, 1.063]. For a student with midterm = 75: ŷ = 15.2 + 0.82(75) = 76.7. 95% CI for mean response: [73.2, 80.2]. 95% PI for an individual student: [61.4, 92.0] — much wider, because any individual student varies around the mean even if we know their midterm score exactly.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Inference for regression completes the connection between sample-based estimates (b₁, b₀) and population-level claims (β₁, β₀). The tests and intervals follow the same logic as all inference — quantifying uncertainty and testing whether observed patterns are too strong to be random. These inference methods apply to every slope in multiple regression, making them the foundation for all model-building and interpretation that follows.
keyPoints: In simple regression, the F-test for the model and the t-test for slope test the same hypothesis — F = t²; in multiple regression they diverge and answer different questions
Prediction intervals are always wider than confidence intervals — they include individual-to-individual variability that CIs for mean responses do not; this distinction is a common exam question
A very small p-value with large n can come from a trivially small slope — always check R² to assess practical importance alongside statistical significance

learn.openingQuestion: Your regression has p < 0.001, R² = 0.85, and your boss is thrilled. You plot the residuals and see a clear U-shaped curve. What does this tell you that R² and p-values didn't — and why does it matter more than those impressive statistics?
learn.anchor: Residuals are what the model can't explain — patterns in them reveal when the model is systematically wrong in predictable ways, and systematic patterns in residuals mean you're making biased predictions regardless of how impressive R² looks.
learn.anchorSummary: Residual patterns reveal assumption violations; a high R² means nothing if residuals show systematic structure.
learn.mechanism:
A curved residual plot means the true relationship is nonlinear. Your model imposes a straight line; the residuals capture the curvature it can't fit. You're not just being imprecise — you're being systematically wrong in the same direction at every value of X. For X values in the middle of the range, you overpredict; at the extremes, you underpredict (or vice versa). High R² with curved residuals means your model captures a lot of variance but misses the actual shape of the relationship.

The four regression assumptions (LINE): Linearity — Y relates to X linearly; check scatterplot and residuals vs. fitted; violation produces a curved residual pattern. Independence — observations don't influence each other; check study design; violated by time series autocorrelation or clustered data. Normality — residuals are normally distributed; check normal Q-Q plot; violation produces an S-curve or bow in the Q-Q plot. Equal variance (homoscedasticity) — residual variance is constant across all X values; check residuals vs. fitted; violation produces a fan-shaped pattern.

Residuals vs. fitted plot patterns: random scatter → assumptions likely met; curved pattern → nonlinearity (add X² or transform); fan shape (spreading outward) → heteroscedasticity (consider log(Y) or weighted least squares); single extreme point → outlier (investigate and decide). Normal Q-Q plot: points on a straight line = normal residuals; deviation at tails = heavy-tailed distribution; S-curve = skewed distribution.

Influential observations: Leverage measures how extreme a point's X value is relative to x̄. Residual measures vertical distance from the line. Cook's Distance combines both — how much the slope changes when the point is removed. Cook's D > 1 or > 4/n flags potentially influential points. An influential point requires BOTH high leverage AND a large residual. High leverage with a small residual (the point falls on or near the line) has minimal influence on the slope.
learn.mechanismSummary: Residuals vs. fitted diagnoses linearity and equal variance; Q-Q plot diagnoses normality; Cook's D identifies influential observations — all four checks are required before trusting any regression results.
learn.boundaryConditions: No regression analysis should be reported without residual plots. Residual analysis guides but doesn't determine transformation choice — theoretical considerations about the relationship matter too. Outlier removal requires justification beyond 'it improved R²' — data errors and genuine extreme observations are both real and must be treated differently. With large n, residuals look approximately normal even without true normality (CLT for residuals), making the normality assumption less critical than equal variance.
learn.example: Regressing house prices (Y) on square footage (X). n = 200. R² = 0.82, p < 0.001. Residuals vs. fitted: fan-shaped — variance increases with fitted value (expensive homes have more price variability). This heteroscedasticity makes standard errors wrong, undermining all inference. Fix: regress log(price) on square footage. After log transformation: residual plot becomes more homoscedastic, Q-Q plot improves. One observation has Cook's D = 0.87 — investigation reveals it's a $50M beachfront estate following different market dynamics. Excluding it with documentation is justified.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Regression diagnostics close the loop: fit a model, examine what it can't explain, and use those unexplained patterns to improve the model or acknowledge its limitations. A model that passes diagnostics is trustworthy; one that fails has systematic biases that R² and p-values cannot reveal or fix. This same diagnostic framework applies in multiple regression — residual plots remain the primary tool regardless of model complexity.
keyPoints: A curved residual plot is the most important signal — it means the model is fundamentally wrong (nonlinear), not just imprecise; high R² does not override this
Fan-shaped residuals (heteroscedasticity) make standard errors wrong and invalidate all inference — address with transformations or robust standard errors before reporting results
High leverage ≠ influential: a high-leverage point that falls near the regression line has minimal influence on the slope; influence requires both extreme X AND large residual

learn.openingQuestion: A study finds students who drink more coffee have higher GPAs — r = 0.38, p = 0.001. When you add hours studied per week to the model, the coffee coefficient drops to near zero and becomes non-significant. Did coffee just disappear from the analysis?
learn.anchor: In multiple regression, each coefficient represents the effect of one predictor holding all others constant — which is why a predictor that looks important in isolation can become negligible when confounders are explicitly controlled.
learn.anchorSummary: Multiple regression partial slopes control for other predictors — that's why coefficients change when variables are added.
learn.mechanism:
Coffee didn't disappear from the analysis — it was never causally there. Students who drink more coffee also study more hours. In simple regression, coffee's coefficient captured the effect of study time that happened to correlate with coffee consumption. When study hours enters the model, it absorbs that confounding effect, and coffee's partial slope drops to near zero. This is exactly what multiple regression is supposed to do: isolate each predictor's unique contribution.

The multiple regression model: Y = β₀ + β₁X₁ + β₂X₂ + ... + βₖXₖ + ε. Each βᵢ is a partial slope — the expected change in Y for a one-unit increase in Xᵢ, holding all other predictors constant. This 'holding constant' interpretation is what makes partial slopes different from simple correlation slopes, and why they change when predictors are added or removed.

The overall F test: H₀: β₁ = β₂ = ... = βₖ = 0 (no linear relationship between Y and any predictor). This omnibus test should be checked first. Individual t-tests: H₀: βᵢ = 0 given all other predictors in the model. A predictor can be significant alone but not in the multiple model (when correlated with others), or non-significant alone but significant in the multiple model (when other predictors reduce error variance).

Adjusted R² = 1 − (1−R²)(n−1)/(n−k−1) penalizes for adding predictors. Adding a useless predictor always slightly increases raw R² but decreases adjusted R². Use adjusted R² to compare models with different numbers of predictors. A counterintuitive partial slope example: predicting house price from square footage (X₁) and number of bedrooms (X₂). The bedroom coefficient can be negative — more bedrooms in the same square footage means smaller rooms, which buyers don't prefer. The partial slope captures within-square-footage comparisons, not the raw correlation with price.
learn.mechanismSummary: Partial slopes show each predictor's unique contribution after controlling for others; adjusted R² penalizes complexity; overall F test must be significant before interpreting individual t-tests.
learn.boundaryConditions: Multiple regression assumes all LINE conditions for simple regression, plus no perfect multicollinearity (predictors cannot be exact linear combinations of each other). High multicollinearity inflates standard errors even when coefficients are individually small, making individual t-tests unreliable. Adding more predictors always increases R² but can decrease predictive accuracy on new data (overfitting). Interaction terms (X₁ × X₂) are needed when the effect of one predictor depends on another — omitting them when they exist causes misspecification.
learn.example: Predicting nutrition score (Y) from unfavorable nutrients score (X₁) and favorable nutrients score (X₂). n = 100 foods. Ŷ = 3.33 + 0.82X₁ + 0.57X₂. Overall F(2,97) = 22.4, p < 0.001. t for X₁: 4.12, p < 0.001. t for X₂: 2.89, p = 0.005. R² = 0.316, R²adj = 0.302. Interpretation of 0.82: for each one-unit increase in unfavorable nutrient score, holding favorable nutrients constant, overall nutrition score increases by 0.82. (Foods high in unfavorable nutrients also tend to have more total nutrients, raising overall score — this counterintuitive direction is only revealed when favorable nutrients are controlled.)
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Multiple regression is the extension of simple regression to complex reality, where outcomes have multiple causes and confounders lurk everywhere. The partial slope interpretation — 'effect of X₁ holding everything else constant' — is what makes regression analysis potentially informative about causation, not just prediction. Every coefficient in a multiple regression model is only interpretable relative to the other predictors included in the model.
keyPoints: Partial slopes change when predictors are added or removed — this is not instability or error; it reflects control for confounding and is the expected, correct behavior of the method
A predictor significant in simple regression may become non-significant in multiple regression when it's correlated with another predictor — this is multicollinearity, not a mistake
Check the overall F test before interpreting individual t-tests — individual predictors can appear significant by chance when the overall model is not

learn.openingQuestion: You add every available predictor to a house price model — 50 variables. R² = 0.94. You test it on 20 new houses and the predictions are terrible. Meanwhile a competitor using just 5 variables predicts well. What happened to your 50-variable model?
learn.anchor: Variable selection methods search for useful predictors, but adding too many predictors memorizes the noise in the training data instead of learning the underlying relationship — producing models that fail on new data.
learn.anchorSummary: Too many predictors overfit training noise; variable selection trades off fit against parsimony to find models that generalize.
learn.mechanism:
R² = 0.94 means the 50-variable model explains 94% of price variability in the training data. But many of those 50 variables found 'relationships' that exist only in this particular dataset by chance. When you apply the model to 20 new houses, those spurious relationships are gone — the model produces poor predictions. This is overfitting: the model is too complex and has fit noise as if it were signal. Adding any random variable to a regression model will always increase R² slightly, even if the variable has no true relationship with Y.

The formal problem: with n observations and k predictors, the model has n−k−1 degrees of freedom for error. As k approaches n, the model memorizes every data point (R² → 1) but generalizes to nothing. A rough guideline: at least 10–20 observations per predictor for a reliable model. With n = 60 and 50 predictors, you have only 9 error df — essentially memorizing the data.

Three automated selection methods: Forward selection starts with no predictors and adds one at a time — whichever most improves fit — until no remaining predictor meets the entry threshold. Backward elimination starts with all predictors and removes one at a time — the least significant — until all remaining meet the exit threshold. Stepwise regression combines both: after each addition, test whether any previously added predictor should be removed.

AIC = −2log(L) + 2k and BIC = −2log(L) + k×log(n) are information criteria that reward fit and penalize complexity. Smaller is better. BIC penalizes more heavily for large n, leading to sparser models. Critical caveat: all automated methods perform multiple implicit tests, inflating Type I error. P-values from stepwise output are NOT interpretable the same way as planned tests — they reflect selection bias and typically appear too significant.
learn.mechanismSummary: Automated selection inflates Type I error; AIC and BIC penalize complexity; adjusted R² or held-out validation should guide model choice, not raw R².
learn.boundaryConditions: Automated selection is sensitive to multicollinearity — correlated predictors compete, and which one enters can be arbitrary. These methods are for exploration, not confirmation. Cross-validation (testing on held-out data) is the gold standard for assessing true predictive performance. Domain knowledge should guide variable inclusion — a predictor known to causally affect Y should not be excluded just because it's non-significant in one sample. Never use the same data for both variable selection and hypothesis testing — this double-dipping invalidates inference.
learn.example: Predicting student GPA from 8 potential predictors: SAT score, HS GPA, study hours, extracurricular activities, number of siblings, birth month, shoe size, social media hours. n = 60 students. Backward elimination removes birth month (p=0.92), shoe size (p=0.84), siblings (p=0.71), extracurricular (p=0.34). Final model: SAT, HS GPA, study hours, social media. R²adj improves from 0.58 (all 8 predictors) to 0.62 (4 predictors) — parsimony wins. The p-values for remaining predictors should be treated as exploratory, not confirmatory, because the selection process tested all 8 predictors.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Variable selection represents the fundamental tension in statistical modeling: fit vs. parsimony. Every model is a simplification; the goal is the simplification that captures real patterns without mistaking noise for signal. No automated method solves this completely — domain knowledge, validation on new data, and honest reporting remain essential. Adjusted R², AIC, and BIC all formalize the same tradeoff: reward fit, penalize complexity.
keyPoints: Stepwise and other automated methods inflate Type I error — p-values for selected variables cannot be interpreted the same way as pre-planned tests; treat all selections as exploratory
Adjusted R², AIC, and BIC all penalize model complexity — prefer them over raw R² when comparing models with different numbers of predictors
High multicollinearity makes automated selection unstable — which of two correlated predictors enters the model can be arbitrary, and the one chosen may not be more important

learn.openingQuestion: Productivity increases as temperature rises from 60°F to 72°F, then collapses above 80°F. A linear regression on this data shows no significant relationship (p = 0.42, R² = 0.02). How did a linear model completely miss a pattern this obvious in the data?
learn.anchor: When the true relationship between X and Y is curved, a linear model averages over the upward and downward portions and finds 'no trend' — adding a squared term lets the model bend to match the data's actual shape.
learn.anchorSummary: Polynomial regression captures curved relationships that linear models average over and miss entirely.
learn.mechanism:
The temperature-productivity result reveals why checking the scatterplot before fitting a regression is mandatory. The upward trend (60–72°F) and downward trend (72–90°F) cancel out when averaged into a single linear slope. The linear model is technically correct — it finds the best straight line through the data — but the best straight line is the wrong model for curved data. A flat slope with p = 0.42 and R² = 0.02 doesn't mean 'no relationship'; it means 'no linear relationship.'

The quadratic regression model: Y = β₀ + β₁X + β₂X² + ε. This model is still 'linear' in a technical sense (linear in parameters β₀, β₁, β₂) but fits a parabola to the data. Testing whether the quadratic term is needed: H₀: β₂ = 0 vs. Hₐ: β₂ ≠ 0. If this t-test is significant, the curved model significantly improves on the linear one.

The hierarchical principle requires keeping X in the model when X² is included, even if X's individual t-test is non-significant. Removing X when X² is retained violates model hierarchy and produces nonsensical results. Interpretation: you cannot interpret β₁ alone in a curvilinear model because the effect of X on Y depends on the current value of X. The vertex of the parabola is at X* = −β₁/(2β₂) — this is the X value where Y is maximized (if β₂ < 0) or minimized (if β₂ > 0). Sign of β₂: negative = inverted U-shape (concave down, peak exists); positive = U-shape (concave up, trough exists).

The diagnostic: after fitting the linear model, a curved pattern in residuals vs. fitted values signals that a quadratic or other nonlinear term is needed. After fitting the quadratic, the residual plot should show no remaining pattern.
learn.mechanismSummary: Add X² to fit curved relationships; test with H₀: β₂=0; keep X in the model always; find the optimal X at vertex = −β₁/(2β₂); sign of β₂ determines curve direction.
learn.boundaryConditions: Polynomial regression can fit any curve closely with enough terms, but complex polynomials overfit badly outside the data range — extrapolation is even more dangerous than in linear regression. The hierarchical principle is mandatory: never include X² without X. Curvilinear regression is one solution to nonlinearity; logarithmic and power transformations are alternatives with better extrapolation properties for some relationships. Prefer theoretical justification (why would this relationship be quadratic?) over pure data-driven curve-fitting.
learn.example: Fertilizer amount (X, kg/hectare) vs. crop yield (Y, tons/hectare). n = 24 plots. Scatterplot shows yield increases then decreases (too much fertilizer burns crops). Linear regression: slope = 0.12, p = 0.31, R² = 0.08. Residuals vs. fitted: clear concave-down curved pattern. Add X²: Ŷ = 2.1 + 0.68X − 0.0031X². Test β₂: t = −4.2, p < 0.001 — significant improvement. R² jumps from 0.08 to 0.64. Optimal fertilizer: X* = −0.68/(2×(−0.0031)) = 110 kg/hectare. The agronomist now has an actionable recommendation that the linear model completely missed.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Curvilinear regression is part of a broader toolkit for handling nonlinearity: polynomial terms, log/power transformations, and nonparametric smoothing. When the scatterplot shows curvature or residuals show a pattern after a linear fit, the straight-line assumption is wrong and must be relaxed. The choice of nonlinear form should be guided by theory about the underlying relationship, not just which one fits best.
keyPoints: A non-significant linear regression on clearly curved data is not evidence of no relationship — it's evidence of no linear relationship; always check the scatterplot and residuals before concluding 'no association'
The hierarchical principle: you must keep X in the model whenever X² is included, regardless of X's individual t-test p-value
The vertex X* = −β₁/(2β₂) gives the optimal X value; this is the most practically important number from a curvilinear model and is frequently tested on exams

learn.openingQuestion: A study of 10,000 people finds 60% of regular coffee drinkers develop cancer vs. 56% of non-drinkers — p = 0.001. Should this change your coffee habits? And what does it mean that a 4-percentage-point difference is 'statistically significant'?
learn.anchor: Chi-square tests determine whether the distribution of one categorical variable depends on the level of another — separating real associations from the random imbalances that occur in any finite sample.
learn.anchorSummary: Chi-square tests whether observed cell counts deviate from what independence would predict.
learn.mechanism:
The cancer-coffee example illustrates the gap between statistical and practical significance. A 4-percentage-point difference (60% vs. 56%) in a study of 10,000 people will easily reach p = 0.001 because large samples detect even tiny differences. Whether a 4% difference is worth changing behavior depends on whether the study is observational (confounders like lifestyle factors could explain everything), what type of cancer, and what the effect sizes are for established risk factors. The chi-square test answered a narrow question — 'is there an association?' — very precisely, while leaving the practically important question unanswered.

The chi-square test statistic: χ² = Σ (O − E)² / E, summed over all cells. O = observed count, E = expected count under independence. Expected counts: E_ij = (Row i total × Column j total) / Grand total. This formula implements the definition of independence: if the two variables are independent, P(row i ∩ column j) = P(row i) × P(column j), which translates to cell counts proportional to marginal totals.

Three types: Goodness-of-fit tests whether one variable follows a specified distribution; df = k − 1 (k categories). Test of independence uses one random sample classified on two variables; df = (r−1)(c−1). Test of homogeneity uses separate random samples from different populations and tests whether the distribution of one variable is the same across populations; df = (r−1)(c−1). Independence and homogeneity use identical formulas but differ in study design — knowing which you're doing matters for interpretation. Required condition: expected count ≥ 5 in every cell.

For a 2×2 treatment table: Drug A vs. placebo, improved vs. not. Observed: Drug/Improved = 70, Drug/Not = 30, Placebo/Improved = 50, Placebo/Not = 50 (totals: 100 each group, 120 improved, 80 not, 200 total). Expected: Drug/Improved = 100×120/200 = 60, Drug/Not = 40, Placebo/Improved = 60, Placebo/Not = 40. χ² = (70−60)²/60 + (30−40)²/40 + (50−60)²/60 + (50−40)²/40 = 1.667 + 2.5 + 1.667 + 2.5 = 8.33. df = (2−1)(2−1) = 1. p = 0.004. Reject H₀ — evidence that treatment and outcome are associated.
learn.mechanismSummary: χ² = Σ(O−E)²/E; expected counts = (row total × column total)/grand total; df = (r−1)(c−1) for two-way tables; all expected counts must be ≥ 5.
learn.boundaryConditions: Chi-square tests association, not causation — always consider confounders in observational data. Large samples guarantee significance for even trivial associations; report Cramér's V = √(χ²/(n × min(r−1, c−1))) as an effect size. Chi-square cannot detect the direction of association — examine row/column proportions. Ordinal categorical variables have more powerful tests than chi-square when ordering matters. Fisher's exact test is used for 2×2 tables when expected counts are below 5.
learn.example: NC State surveys 400 students on class format preference (in-person/hybrid/online) by major (business/engineering/humanities). df = (3−1)(3−1) = 4. χ² = 78.3, p < 0.001. Strong evidence that major and format preference are associated — engineering students prefer hybrid while humanities students prefer online. Cramér's V = √(78.3/(400×2)) = 0.31, a medium effect. The pattern, not just the p-value, tells the substantive story.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Contingency tables formalize the question 'do these two categorical variables move together?' The chi-square statistic measures how far the observed table is from what perfect independence would look like. Understanding the expected count calculation is the key to understanding what independence means for categorical data — it's the same concept that underlies all conditional probability calculations.
keyPoints: df for independence/homogeneity = (rows−1)(columns−1); df for goodness-of-fit = k−1; these are different formulas commonly confused on exams

Expected count condition: ALL cells need E ≥ 5, not just the total sample size — small categories fail this condition even in large datasets; combine categories or use Fisher's exact test when violated
Chi-square tests whether an association exists, not its direction or magnitude; examine row/column proportions and report Cramér's V for effect size alongside the p-value

learn.openingQuestion: A new medication reduces stroke risk from 4% to 2%. The company advertises 'a 50% reduction in stroke risk.' Your doctor says 'your risk drops from 4 to 2 in 100 patients.' Both statements are mathematically correct. Why do they feel completely different — and which one should determine whether you take the medication?
learn.anchor: Categorical data analysis forces precision about how we measure and compare proportions — because relative risk, absolute risk reduction, and odds ratios can tell completely different stories from identical data, and the choice of measure shapes decisions.
learn.anchorSummary: Relative vs. absolute risk measures can tell opposite stories from the same data; choose the measure that matches the decision at hand.
learn.mechanism:
The stroke medication example is how pharmaceutical companies legally advertise drugs. A 50% relative risk reduction sounds dramatic. A 2-percentage-point absolute risk reduction tells you something concrete: out of 100 patients treated for a year, 2 fewer have strokes. The number needed to treat (NNT = 1/ARR = 1/0.02 = 50) means you must treat 50 patients for a year to prevent 1 stroke. Whether that's worth the cost and side effects depends on the individual — but the framing shapes the decision powerfully.

Relative risk (RR) = P(outcome | exposed) / P(outcome | unexposed). From a 2×2 table: RR = [a/(a+b)] / [c/(c+d)]. RR = 1 means no association. RR > 1 means exposure increases risk. RR < 1 means exposure decreases risk. Requires knowing the population at risk in each group — possible in cohort studies and clinical trials, not in case-control studies.

Odds ratio (OR) = (a/b)/(c/d) = ad/bc. The odds ratio approximates relative risk when outcomes are rare (< 10% baseline). OR ≠ RR when outcomes are common — OR systematically overstates RR for common outcomes. Case-control studies must use OR because RR is undefined — you sampled on outcome, not exposure. Logistic regression coefficients are log-odds; e^β gives the OR.

Absolute risk reduction (ARR) = P(outcome | control) − P(outcome | treated). NNT = 1/ARR. From the stroke example: 1,000 patients per group. Treatment: 20 strokes (2%). Control: 40 strokes (4%). RR = 20/40 = 0.50 (50% relative reduction). ARR = 0.04 − 0.02 = 0.02 (2 percentage point absolute reduction). NNT = 50. OR = (20×960)/(40×980) = 0.49 ≈ RR because strokes are rare (< 10% baseline).
learn.mechanismSummary: RR = ratio of outcome rates (needs cohort data); OR = ratio of odds (works for case-control); ARR = absolute difference; NNT = 1/ARR; OR ≈ RR only when outcomes are rare.
learn.boundaryConditions: Relative risk requires knowing population denominators — impossible in case-control studies. Odds ratios are used when sampling is on the outcome. OR approximates RR only for rare outcomes (< 10% baseline rate) — for common outcomes, OR systematically overstates the relative risk, sometimes dramatically. NNT depends on baseline risk: a medication with NNT = 50 in a high-risk population may have NNT = 500 in a low-risk population, even with the same relative risk. All measures describe association, not causation.
learn.example: Case-control study on lung cancer. 200 cases (lung cancer), 200 controls (no cancer). Exposure: heavy smoking (yes/no). Observed: Cancer/Smoking = 150, Cancer/NoSmoking = 50; Control/Smoking = 50, Control/NoSmoking = 150. OR = (150×150)/(50×50) = 22,500/2,500 = 9.0. Heavy smokers have 9 times the odds of lung cancer compared to non-smokers. 95% CI for OR: compute ln(9) ± 1.96 × √(1/150 + 1/50 + 1/50 + 1/150) = 2.197 ± 1.96 × 0.238 = [1.626, 2.769] on log scale = [5.1, 15.9] on OR scale. CI excludes 1 — significant association. RR cannot be computed from case-control data.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Categorical data analysis connects all the statistical methods for non-continuous outcomes: from simple proportions and chi-square tests to logistic regression with multiple predictors. The choice of measure (RR vs. OR vs. ARR) is not purely a statistical question — it's a communication decision with real consequences for how people understand and act on evidence. Logistic regression extends these ideas to multiple predictors, producing OR-based summaries of complex categorical relationships.
keyPoints: Relative risk sounds more dramatic than absolute risk from the same data — always report both and compute NNT for clinical or policy context; relative measures alone are routinely misleading

Odds ratio ≈ relative risk only when the outcome is rare (< 10% baseline rate); for common outcomes, OR systematically overstates RR — this is a frequent exam trap and a real-world communication error
Case-control studies must use OR because you sampled on outcome — RR is undefined; logistic regression coefficients are log-odds, so exponentiate (e^β) to get the odds ratio
